instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
All you need to know about the beneficiary in a life insurance policy
As we all know, a life insurance policy is intended to financially protect the heirs or dependents of the policyholder in the event of the untimely demise of the insured. To avoid any confusion, the policyholder is expected to declare the beneficiary in the policy. Such an insurance beneficiary is also called a nominee unless the policy is assigned to someone else. The policyholder is the only one with the right to change the nominated person at any point. Here are other nuances about nominating a trusted person for your life insurance policy.
Choice of beneficiary:The Government of India introduced the concept of beneficial nominees, where the legal successors of the insured are not allowed to claim death benefits unless they are assigned as beneficiaries in the life insurance plan. The beneficiary of life insurance can be either an individual or an organisation. The person nominated as a beneficiary can be a member of your family, a friend or a colleague. Any NGO can be nominated as the beneficiary of the policy. If the insurance beneficiary is a minor, then there is a need to assign a custodian. The minor would receive the benefits upon attaining 18 years of age.
Types of beneficiaries:There are two types of insurance beneficiaries based on the ability to change the beneficiary, namely:
Irrevocable: Under the irrevocable mode of nominating the beneficiary, once done, the beneficiary for life insurance cannot be changed. For any changes in beneficiary, there is a need to get the currently nominated insurance beneficiary to agree to forfeit their right for benefits from the life insurance. This happens in the case of an absolute assignment, wherein the assignor transfers complete rights of the policy to the assignee and it is executed through an endorsement on the original policy bond. However, in case of conditional assignment, once the condition is fulfilled, the policy is re-assigned back to the poicyholder.
Revocable: Here the policyholder can change the beneficiary at any given point in time for any reason that the insured considers valid. The insurer is obligated to adhere to the request and make necessary arrangements to change the beneficiary of the policy. This happens under nomination wherein the policyholder can keep changing the beneficiary of the plan at any point of time.This change can happen through email or a simple letter to the insurer and do not involve any legal proceeding.
Apart from this, there is the primary and contingent beneficiary. The primary beneficiary is one who is nominated as the first choice to receive benefits. In case the primary beneficiary meets an untimely death, then the contingent beneficiary would receive the benefits.
Policy without beneficiary:
There are situations where the policy would not have a definite beneficiary. Here are the various recourse that the insurer would opt for:
In the event of the untimely death of the life insured during the policy term, without having nominated a beneficiary, the insurer is obligated to follow certain mandates as required in such a situation.
If you have done your estate planning, then the insurers would follow the Indian Succession Act, 1925. The sum assured would be divided to the heirs as mentioned in the will, in the proportion indicated in the will. In this scenario, the court has to issue a succession certificate. In addition, the beneficiaries will be required to provide proof of indemnity or waiver of legal proof to the insurer.
Under circumstances where no will assigns someone as a beneficiary, the death benefit is paid out to the immediate heirs. Here the immediate family members, spouse, children and parents are considered heirs.
However, as a ideal practice, one should opt for a nominee of a plan, especially if it is taken on single life so that the proceeds of the insurance plan is not left unclaimed.
What is an “assignment” of life insurance beneficiary?
Assignment of life insurance policy is when the rights of the insurance policy are transferred to someone else. The assignment can be an absolute assignment, i.e. irreversible, or conditional, i.e., i.e. till the conditions are not fulfilled, such as a loan. So, when you pledge your life insurance policy or take a loan against the same, the policy is conditionally assigned to the lender. Once the loan is successfully repaid, the policy is transferred back to the policyholder. However, in the absolute assignment, the rights of the policy are permanently transferred to the  insurance beneficiary and cannot be revoked. The person who assigns the policy, i.e. the policyholder, is called the assignor and the person to whom the policy is assigned is called the assignee.What is “nomination” in a life insurance plan?
The process of naming someone who is beneficiary of the plan, in case the life insured dies within the policy tenure is called “Nomination” and the person who is nominated is called the “Nominee”. You can choose a nominee at the time of policy inception or at any point of time.
The policy bond should be signed in the presence of a witness, thereby establishing the authenticity of beneficiary and nominee in the life insurance policy.
Change in beneficiary
You may be required to change the nominee as the beneficiary on your life insurance policy if you have any triggering event or life event such as: getting divorced, getting married, childbirth, or death of beneficiary etc. It is important to effect the change in beneficiary immediately upon such triggering events, to ensure that the funds reach the correct beneficiary who is financially dependent upon you.
As indicated earlier, under the revocable policy, the insurance beneficiary can be changed. There is no limit on the number of times the beneficiary may be changed through a simple nomination form. At all times, the existing and proposed beneficiaries have to be made aware of the change in nomination, this would help avoid any form of confusion in the future.
However, if the policy is assigned to another person or identity, the same is considered to benomination has been made irrevocable, then the change in beneficiary is a more complicated process. The existing beneficiary has to forfeit their right to benefits from the life insurance policy. Until such time changes, the beneficiary will not be allowed.
Basically, the change in nominee is a very simple procedure, wherein the insurer can be intimated and an application form to that effect is signed by the insured or an email. Upon acceptance of the application, the beneficiary will be changed.
In a nutshell
Nominating an insurance beneficiary is a very important step whilst acquiring a life insurance policy. It is imperative to ensure that the benefit from the life insurance policy protects the near and dear ones financially. It has to be a very well-thought-out process, it is suggested that you make a thorough assessment before you assign a beneficiary to your life insurance policy.
